The wife must fulfill her duty to her husband, even if he does not obey God's word. We often see how people who do not believe watch the actions and lives of those who claim to be religious. Wearing certain clothes is not forbidden, but vanity and extravagance in adornment are. Religious people should make sure their behavior matches what they profess. But few know the right amount and limits of essential things like food and clothing! Unless poverty restricts us, almost everyone desires something beyond what is good for them. People are more indebted to their humble circumstances than to a humble attitude; many will spend their time and money on trivialities. The apostle instructs Christian women to put on something that cannot decay, something that beautifies the soul—the graces of God’s Holy Spirit. A true Christian's main concern should be ordering his own spirit. This will do more to strengthen affection and earn a husband's respect than carefully chosen clothes or fashionable attire, combined with an irritable and quarrelsome temper. Christians ought to do their duty to one another willingly and in obedience to God’s command. Wives should submit to their husbands not from fear, but from a desire to do what is right and please God. A husband's duty to his wife includes giving her respect, maintaining her authority, protecting her, and trusting her. They are heirs together of all the blessings of this life and that which is to come, and should live peacefully one with another. Prayer sweetens their conversations. And it is not enough for them to pray as a family; husband and wife should also pray together privately, along with their children. Those who know prayer find such great joy in it that they will not let anything stop them. To pray often, live righteously; and to live righteously, be much in prayer.

Although Christians cannot always agree completely, they should have compassion for one another and love as brothers. If anyone wants to live comfortably on earth or possess eternal life in heaven, he must control his tongue from wicked, abusive, or deceitful words. He must abandon evil actions and do all the good he can, seeking peace with everyone. For God, who is all-knowing and everywhere present, watches over the righteous and cares for them. No one could or should harm those who followed Christ’s example, who is perfect goodness and did good to others as his followers.

We honor God before others when our actions invite and encourage them to glorify and honor him. What was the basis of their hope? We should be able to defend our religion with gentleness, in reverence for God. There is no room for other fears where this great reverence exists; it does not disturb anyone. A conscience is good when it performs its duty well. That person is in a sad condition on whom sin and suffering meet: sin makes suffering extreme, hopeless, and destructive. Surely it is better to suffer for doing what is right than for doing wrong, whatever our natural impatience may suggest. Christ’s example shows us how to be patient under suffering. In the case of Christ's suffering, he who knew no sin suffered instead of those who knew no righteousness. The blessed purpose and result of Christ’s sufferings were to reconcile us to God and bring us to eternal glory. He was put to death in respect of his human nature, but was raised by the power of the Holy Spirit. If Christ could not be freed from suffering, why should Christians expect to be? God carefully notices the means and advantages people have had in all ages. Regarding the old world, Christ sent his Spirit and gave warning through Noah. But although God’s patience waits long, it will eventually end. And the spirits of disobedient sinners, as soon as they leave their bodies, are handed over to hell, where those who rejected Noah's warning now are, and from which there is no redemption. Noah's salvation in the ark on the water, which carried him above the floods, showed what would happen when all true believers are saved. That temporary salvation by the ark was a symbol of the eternal salvation of believers through baptism with the Holy Spirit. To prevent misunderstandings, the apostle explains what he means by saving baptism; not the outward ceremony of washing with water, which in itself does nothing more than cleanse the body, but that baptism where the water is a sign. Not the outward ritual, but when a person, through the renewal by the Spirit, is able to repent and confess faith and commit to a new life, uprightly and as if in God’s presence. Let us be careful not to rely on external practices. Let us learn to view God's ordinances spiritually and seek their spiritual effect and work on our consciences. Many want to reduce all religion to outward things. But many who were baptized and regularly attended religious services have remained without Christ, died in their sins, and are now beyond help. Do not rest until you are cleansed by the Spirit of Christ and the blood of Christ. His resurrection from the dead is what assures us of purification and peace.
